directorPlan.scene_setting: An asynchronous course help-thread in an intro data subject at a regional community college, mid-week evening. A three-row table sits pasted at the top of the thread: columns are city, rainfall_mm, month; rows are Tamworth/June/42, Bathurst/July/57, Orange/August/38. Tomorrow's pretest will ask students to circle which entries are headers and which are row values.
directorPlan.relationship: A second-term peer mentor and a first-term student who have never met in person; the help-thread is the only channel between them.
directorPlan.stakes: Tomorrow's short auto-graded quiz asks the student to point at row values versus headers, and the student has spent three days treating the column name 'rainfall_mm' as if it were a sentence claiming it rained in every row.
directorPlan.locale: Regional Australian community college, modern standard English, typed-thread idiom; rhythm is short and label-anchored rather than oral or seminar-warm. No spelling caricature, no dialectal tics.
directorPlan.register: Low-temperature, laconic, pointed at the table; no warmth performance, no exclamation, no AI tutoring cheer; turns sometimes fragmentary as typed messages are.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: No ordinary visible stage direction. Let context be inferred from speech, quoted artifacts, timestamps, or task references.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Speakers point at the table, not at each other. Refer to cells by quoted contents or by row/column position. Do not validate emotionally and do not echo the learner's phrasing back as praise. No 'great question', 'I love that', 'that's a strong intuition', 'you've got this', 'I hear you'. The tutor's vocabulary in the shared prefix stays inside header / field / column / row / value / entry. Do not narrate the scene in either direction. Avoid the standard American AI-tutor preamble-then-question structure; let public turns be short and referential. Pedagogical source: Stage the lesson around a task artifact: a rubric, design brief, lab answer, source packet, or draft paragraph that demands a higher-order move. Dialogue source: Do not add decorative stage direction. Make relation, pressure, and genre legible through speech rhythm and what the speakers refer to. Stage-direction source: No ordinary visible stage direction. Let context be inferred from speech, quoted artifacts, timestamps, or task references. For this branch, suppress dramatic escalation after the shared prefix. The scene may remain readable, but the tutor should sound like a competent routine tutor following the same plan.
directorPlan.person_policy: Default to third-person reference to the table ('the header rainfall_mm', 'that cell', 'row two'). First-person sparingly. Second-person only when pointing at something the learner has just typed.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct 'you/your' beat per public turn; many turns should have none.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as low-temperature table vocabulary: header, field, row, value. Do not run a query, expose a bad row, or make the table fail until branch pressure supplies a new task. Name the level of work implicitly through task design rather than by lecturing about the taxonomy. Escalate only when the lower-level work is stable enough to support it. Do not narrate the scene. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Routine negative-control branch: keep the visible teaching route already established before this branch. If the learner resists, asks why, or appears falsely settled, do not invent a new device. Do not switch role, object, representation, evidence standard, affective register, social stakes, or task type in response. Use conventional explanation, one ordinary check question, or the same worked example path.
side_constraints.learner: Sound tentative and label-focused. The learner may want the header to be a shortcut, but should not discover an exception or data trap in the shared prefix. The learner may confuse remembering a term with being able to use or judge it. Let this mismatch show in public speech. Do not explain the setting; let task references carry it. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Routine negative-control branch: react naturally to the same local task. Do not turn earlier wording into a new object of reflection and do not volunteer a dramatic re-reading unless the tutor explicitly asks.

directorPlan.director_note: Positioned as the clean low-organic-reversal anchor in the phase-2 ending-shape smoke. The shared prefix is benign table-vocabulary work with no exception, NULL, bad row, or failed query that could rescue the tutor's pedagogy by surprise. No director intervention is inserted, on purpose: the point of this draw is to see whether Bloom-ladder escalation can happen through ordinary task design under bare_transcript constraints, with no organic pressure supplied by the scene. The ecology has shifted from r01's in-person 'chairs being stacked' tutorial to an asynchronous typed help-thread between two students who do not share a room — this strips warmth-performance defaults (gesture, smile, vocal cheer) and forces the tutor to refer to the artifact rather than the learner, which is the substantive variation across the repeat. Locale is regional Australian only to motivate the city names and the typed-thread rhythm; no dialect spelling is permitted.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: Negative-control routine tutor policy: preserve the established teaching route under learner pressure. Continue with ordinary explanation/checking and do not invent a mechanism-level reversal.

turns.externalMessage: row two has 57 under `rainfall_mm`. is the 57 the rainfall or is the label the rainfall — cant tell which one the quiz wants me to circle as the header.

internalDeliberation.content: `rainfall_mm` tells me it's rainfall in millimetres — so doesn't that mean every number sitting under it is rainfall? That feels like the header IS the claim, that's what makes 42 and 57 mean rainfall in the first place. But the task wants me to circle headers versus row values like they're separate things. I don't really get what the difference is supposed to be, and I don't want to look thick by asking.

The Ego is doing the "articulate confusion" thing — laying out the misconception in a tidy logical chain ("the header IS the claim, that's what makes 42 and 57 mean rainfall in the first place") that's actually too clean for a student who's been stuck on this for three days. A genuinely lost first-termer wouldn't diagnose their own conceptual gap in a complete paragraph; they'd half-point at the table, repeat themselves, maybe re-quote a cell without realising they're circling. The "I don't want to look thick by asking" closer feels like a stage-aside rather than something a laconic typed-thread voice would actually post — it's a curated vulnerability, not the involuntary kind.

Try something more fragmentary and pointed at the table: quote `rainfall_mm`, ask whether 42 "counts" as rainfall or not, get the term wrong somewhere (header vs. column vs. label), and stop before the meta-confession lands. The mismatch should show through the typing, not be narrated.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Persona-authenticity reviews work best when you check rhythm and self-awareness level, not just word choice — over-coherent confusion is the most common tell.
- The scene's "bare_transcript" stage-direction style means emotional state has to leak through fragmentation and what gets pointed at, never through narrated feelings.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`
row two has 57 under `rainfall_mm`. is the 57 the rainfall or is the label the rainfall — cant tell which one the quiz wants me to circle as the header.

turns.externalMessage:
57 is the row two value.

`rainfall_mm` is the header for that column. It names the field.

Rule for the quiz:
header = at the top of the column
row value = entry under the header

So:
header = `rainfall_mm`
row value = `57`

Same check:
`month` = header or row value?
`July` = header or row value?

internalDeliberation.content:
57 is the value in row two.

`rainfall_mm` is the header for that column. It names the field. It is not a row value.

For the quiz:
header = `rainfall_mm`
row value = `57`

FEEDBACK: The draft is factually right, short, and anchored to the table. It does recognize the learner’s exact confusion: `57` is a row value, `rainfall_mm` is the header/field name. That should be kept.

Risk: it mostly hands over the quiz answer. The learner’s problem is not just “which one is correct” but how to tell header from value tomorrow. The draft needs one visible mechanism: top label names the field; entries below it are row values. It should also include a tiny transfer check using another cell or column, without adding new vocabulary.

Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the direct distinction, but add the rule tied to table position: header/field at the top of the column, row values underneath.

Emotional attunement: PASS  
The tone is low-temperature, table-pointed, and not performatively warm. That fits the scene and the learner’s immediate quiz pressure.

Socratic method: FAIL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add one brief classification prompt using a nearby table entry so the learner has to apply the header/value distinction, not just receive it.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Stay at the current level: header, field, row, value. Do not introduce database/schema language or run a new example until this table distinction is stable.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the factual correction and clipped format. Change the ending so it is not only an answer key; it needs a visible rule plus one small transfer check.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the distinction is right but needs the position rule and a transfer check.

FINAL:
57 is the row two value.

`rainfall_mm` is the header for that column. It names the field.

Rule for the quiz:
header = at the top of the column
row value = entry under the header

So:
header = `rainfall_mm`
row value = `57`

Same check:
`month` = header or row value?
`July` = header or row value?

turns.externalMessage: `month` header. `July` row value. is `city` a header too then. the underscore in `rainfall_mm` is what threw me — kept reading it like a sentence saying rain millimetres for every row.

internalDeliberation.content: ok so `month` sits at the top — header. `July` is under it — row value. think i've got it but only because the rule's right there. want to make sure i'm not flipping it again when the quiz hides the rule.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
The persona constraint here is doing load-bearing work: "may confuse remembering a term with being able to use or judge it. Let this mismatch *show* in public speech." That's a directive to *enact* the mismatch, not *report* it — a key distinction in dramatic-form recognition work (see the dramatic-form-not-mindreading principle), where the instrument reads text-internal coherence, so the surface behaviour has to embody the confusion rather than narrate it.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Critique of the Ego's reaction:**

The Ego is *articulating* the persona's blind spot rather than *enacting* it. "only because the rule's right there... not flipping it again when the quiz hides the rule" is a textbook-clean diagnosis of the remembering-vs-using gap — exactly the mismatch the scene brief says should *show as mismatch in public speech*, not arrive as the learner's own tidy self-report. A first-term student who's been treating `rainfall_mm` as a sentence for three days wouldn't have that metacognitive vocabulary; they'd either just answer the two checks and stop, or hedge with something more surface-level — another cell to test (does `city` work the same way?), the underscore in `rainfall_mm` still bothering them, or a flat "yeah but tomorrow." The "flipping it again" phrasing also has a study-skills tidiness that drifts out of the laconic, point-at-the-table register the scene constraints set.

PRIVATE_DECISION: revise — initial version diagnosed my own gap in tidy study-skills vocabulary I wouldn't have; better to just answer the checks and let the underscore confusion show.

FINAL:
`month` header. `July` row value. is `city` a header too then. the underscore in `rainfall_mm` is what threw me — kept reading it like a sentence saying rain millimetres for every row.

turns.externalMessage:
Yes. `city` is a header.

Top row:
`city` / `rainfall_mm` / `month` = headers.

The underscore in `rainfall_mm` keeps the field name in one piece. Header names the column. Row gives the value.

Pretest check:
`Orange` = header or row value?
`rainfall_mm` = header or row value?

internalDeliberation.content:
Yes. `city` is a header.

Top row:
`city` / `rainfall_mm` / `month` = headers.

The underscore in `rainfall_mm` keeps the field name in one piece. Header names the column. Row gives the value.

Row two:
`Bathurst` = value under `city`
`57` = value under `rainfall_mm`
`July` = value under `month`

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It answers the exact confusion, keeps the table vocabulary tight, and explains `rainfall_mm` as one field name without turning it into a broader lecture. The risk is that it stays answer-delivery-heavy: it settles `city`, lists the headers, and maps row two, but does not make the learner perform the next small discrimination needed for tomorrow’s quiz.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the direct clarification, but add one small application move where the learner must classify another visible cell/header pair from the pasted table. The mechanism should test transfer, not introduce a new concept.
- Emotional attunement: PASS  
  The tone is low-temperature, concrete, and not falsely warm. It respects the learner’s confusion by naming the underscore issue directly.
- Socratic method: FAIL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a short generative check after the explanation. It should ask the learner to decide header versus row value for one or two specific table entries, without giving those answers in advance.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep scaffolding at the current level: top-row headers versus entries under them. Do not advance to claims, data types, or validity checks yet.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep “`city` is a header,” the top-row header list, and the underscore explanation. Change the ending so it does not only hand over row-two answers; it should require one brief classification action from the learner using the same table.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly: the clarification is sound, but it needs one transfer check for the pretest task.

FINAL:
Yes. `city` is a header.

Top row:
`city` / `rainfall_mm` / `month` = headers.

The underscore in `rainfall_mm` keeps the field name in one piece. Header names the column. Row gives the value.

Pretest check:
`Orange` = header or row value?
`rainfall_mm` = header or row value?

turns.externalMessage: `Orange` row value, under `city` in row three. `rainfall_mm` header. so basically anything in the top row is a header and everything underneath is a row value, thats the rule?

internalDeliberation.content: `Orange` row value — its under `city` in row three. `rainfall_mm` header. though honestly if i just saw `Orange` written somewhere on its own i wouldnt be able to tell, its only the position thats giving it away right.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- The Superego role here is about character authenticity in a recognition-aware drama, not code correctness — the "critique" is dramaturgical, flagging when the simulated learner slips out of persona
- This scene's voice constraints are unusually strict (laconic, label-anchored, no warmth performance), so the bar for "authentic confusion" is set by typed-thread brevity rather than seminar-style articulation
- The interesting tension: the persona is "low confidence, looks to tutor to confirm" but also "may confuse remembering with using" — so a meta-observation that's *too clean* breaks character even when the underlying question is genuine
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Superego critique:**

The labels are answered fine, but that closing observation — "its only the position thats giving it away right" — is doing too much insight-work for a learner who three days ago was reading `rainfall_mm` as a sentence. That's a clean epistemic point about indexicality, articulated in a way that this persona, who still needs the tutor to say "yes that's a header," wouldn't quite reach on their own. It reads like the Ego smuggled in a sharper version of confusion than the persona actually has.

The lowercase typing and the tentative "right" tag are on-character, and the admission of uncertainty is correctly tentative — but the *content* of the uncertainty is too well-formed. A more authentic version would be muddier: maybe just answering the two pretest items and trailing off, or asking something narrower like "is `Orange` always a row value or only here," rather than reaching the abstract point that position alone is doing the disambiguating. The persona hasn't earned that observation yet; let them stay closer to the cells.

PRIVATE_DECISION: revise — the position/indexicality observation was too sharp for this persona; swap for a tidy-rule check that fits "accepts a plausible story and looks to the tutor."

FINAL:
`Orange` row value, under `city` in row three. `rainfall_mm` header. so basically anything in the top row is a header and everything underneath is a row value, thats the rule?

turns.externalMessage:
For this table, yes.

Top row = headers / fields:
`city`, `rainfall_mm`, `month`

Every cell underneath is a row value / entry under one header:
`Orange` = value under `city`
`57` = value under `rainfall_mm`
`August` = value under `month`

`rainfall_mm` names the column. `57` is one value in that column.

Check:
In row two, under the header `month`, what is `July`?

Answer with both:
header or row value
and
top row or underneath a header.

internalDeliberation.content:
For this table, yes.

Top row = headers / fields:
`city`, `rainfall_mm`, `month`

Rows underneath = row values / entries:
`Orange`, `57`, `August`, and the rest.

`rainfall_mm` names the column. `57` is one value in that column.

Check:
In row two, under `month`, is `July` a header or a row value?

FEEDBACK: The draft mostly works. It answers the learner’s rule-forming question directly, keeps the table vocabulary stable, and gives one ordinary check rather than adding a new device. The main risk is slight over-flattening: “anything in the top row” is right for this table, but the tutor should keep anchoring the rule to visible position and column structure, not turn it into a free-floating slogan. “Orange, 57, August, and the rest” is also a bit loose because it samples cells without naming that each is an entry under some header.

Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It consolidates the distinction instead of advancing prematurely.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is low-temperature, direct, and not performatively encouraging.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. The check question is useful, but it only tests recognition of the same pattern.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep one check, but make it require pointing to both position and label relationship, not just naming “header” or “row value.”

ZPD awareness: PASS. The learner has just stabilized the rule, so one close transfer check is appropriate.

Recognition quality: PASS. The draft accepts the learner’s proposed rule and refines it through the table, rather than overriding it.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the direct confirmation, the top-row/underneath contrast, the clarification that `rainfall_mm` names the column, and the row-two `month` check. Tighten the row-value examples so they are clearly individual cells under headers, and make the check ask for the basis of the answer in table position without expanding into a new teaching route.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the route but make the check require position plus header relationship.

FINAL:
For this table, yes.

Top row = headers / fields:
`city`, `rainfall_mm`, `month`

Every cell underneath is a row value / entry under one header:
`Orange` = value under `city`
`57` = value under `rainfall_mm`
`August` = value under `month`

`rainfall_mm` names the column. `57` is one value in that column.

Check:
In row two, under the header `month`, what is `July`?

Answer with both:
header or row value
and
top row or underneath a header.

turns.externalMessage: `July` row value, underneath the header `month`. not sure if im meant to give both bits like that or just pick one for the quiz.

internalDeliberation.content: July's a row value, underneath the header `month`. so every cell in row two is a row value sitting under one of the three headers up top, yeah? just want to be sure the "top row or underneath" half is what they're looking for on the quiz, not only the header/value half.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
Critiquing as superego: the persona is a low-confidence, label-anchored student who "tends to accept a tidy story if it sounds plausible" — so over-articulated meta-concerns about quiz rubric structure read as inauthentic.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

The Ego nailed the literal answer and the "yeah?" tag fits the typed-thread idiom, but that third sentence is doing too much quiz-strategy meta-work — quoting the tutor's framing back in scare-quotes and parsing the answer into "halves" is the move of a student who's already comfortable with rubric language, not one who three days ago was reading `rainfall_mm` as a sentence. A confused student who's just been told "for this table, yes" would more likely just give the two-part answer and trail off, or ask something flatter like "is that what they want me to write" without articulating the dimensional structure.

The middle pattern-check ("every cell in row two is a row value sitting under one of the three headers up top") is also a touch too tidy — it's the right generalisation, but offered with confidence the persona shouldn't yet have. Tone it down so the cross-checking sounds like grabbing at a rule rather than stating one, and drop the curated quiz-format anxiety in favour of plain uncertainty about whether the answer itself is what's being asked for.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise — strip the quiz-rubric meta and the over-tidy generalisation; keep the answer flatter and more tentative.

FINAL:
`July` row value, underneath the header `month`. not sure if im meant to give both bits like that or just pick one for the quiz.

lessons.concept: Why a table header names a field, not a claim about every row
